title: Knight Forks the Royals
setup: Black's king on e8 and queen on d5 are both within one knight-jump of f6.
hint: Look for a square the knight can land on that hits **both** the king and the queen.
explanation:
## The Idea

A textbook **family fork**. The square {{f6}} touches e8 (the king) and d5 (the queen) at the same time — the classic knight pattern of attacking two pieces two squares apart in an L-shape.

After {{Nf6+}}:

- **Capture?** The king on e8 isn't adjacent to f6 (it's two squares diagonally), so {{Kxf6}} is illegal. The queen on d5 also can't take f6 — d5 to f6 is a knight pattern, not a queen line.
- **Block?** A knight check is never blockable.
- **Run?** The king must move (Kd7, Kf7, Kd8, Kf8), and on the next move White plays {{Nxd5}}, winning the queen for nothing.

## Pattern to Memorize

> When the enemy king and queen are **two squares apart on a diagonal** (like d5 and e8 — but mirrored: e8 and d5 are on a knight L), the knight can usually fork them from the square in between.
